perturbation.original_text: 1 Non- disclosure Agreement Entrusting party ( Party A): Recipient ( Party B): Shenzhen Artop Inno Co. Ltd (Hereinafter: “Recipient”) Both Entrusting party and the Recipient will also be together referred to as the “Parties”. WHEREAS: a) Entrusting party ( Party A) and Recipient ( Party B ) Propose to conduct discussions and negotiations regarding the cooperative project. Exchanging of relevant business and technical information is required for the ongoing business discussions or cooperation between Entrusting party and Recipient with respect to ( Project name) _ ___, this agreement is entered into by and between Entrusting party and Recipient through friendly consultations and under the principle of mutual benefit and joint development.
perturbation.changed_text: 1 Non- disclosure Agreement Transferor ( Party A): Transferee ( Party B): Shenzhen Artop Inno Co. Ltd (Hereinafter: “Transferee”) Both Transferor and the Transferee will also be together referred to as the “Parties”. WHEREAS: a) Transferor ( Party A) and Transferee ( Party B ) Propose to conduct discussions and negotiations regarding the cooperative project. Exchanging of relevant business and technical information is required for the ongoing business discussions or cooperation between Transferor and Transferee with respect to ( Project name) _ ___, this agreement is entered into by and between Transferor and Transferee through friendly consultations and under the principle of mutual benefit and joint development.
perturbation.explanation: The terms 'Entrusting party' and 'Recipient' are replaced with 'Transferor' and 'Transferee,' respectively. While the document proceeds to define the Recipient, there's no explicit indication that 'Transferor' and 'Transferee' are now the operative terms. This contradiction occurs when later sections still refer to 'Entrusting party' and 'Recipient', leading to confusion about which party holds which obligations throughout the document. Particularly, there's contradiction created between section 1 and the other sections which weren't updated with the new terms.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.1 Both Entrusting party(Party A) or Recipient(Party B) have the right to preserve necessary confidential information, so as to make use of which in implementing bindi ng laws, regulations, and obligations under their cooperative programs.
perturbation.justification: The change replaces 'Entrusting party' and 'Recipient' with 'Transferor' and 'Transferee'. The contradicted text, section 2.1, still refers to 'Entrusting party' and 'Recipient', creating a direct conflict because the agreement now operates with different terms that are not consistent throughout the document. This misaligned terminology causes a strong in-text contradiction, because the definitions and responsibilities of Entrusting party/Recipient are not directly transferrable to Transferor/Transferee, making it unclear who is obligated to do what, which fits the definition of Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Definition of Confidential Information Confidential information refer s to date and information with respect to relevant businesses and technical information, whether in written or other forms, that have been disclosed by either Entrusting party or Recipient with clear label or designation of "Confidential information" (here inafter referred to as "Confidential information"), excluding the following date and information:
perturbation.changed_text: Definition of Protected Information Protected information refer s to date and information with respect to relevant businesses and technical information, whether in written or other forms, that have been disclosed by either Transferor or Transferee with clear label or designation of "Protected information" (here inafter referred to as "Protected information"), excluding the following date and information:
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Confidential Information' to 'Protected Information' introduces a term that is not consistently used throughout the document. While the new definition explicitly uses the terms Transferor and Transferee as defined on the first perturbation, it introduces an in-text contradiction by inconsistently using the term confidential information in other sections. Section 3 is directly affected as it refers to disclosure of 'confidential information', without acknowledging 'Protected Information'
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.1 Disclosure of the confidential information by either Entrusting party(Party A) or Recipient (Party B )to the other party or its representatives sha ll not be construed to constitute an assignment or grant to the other party or its representatives of the rights and interests in relation to its trade secrets , trademarks , patents , know -how or any other intellectual property , nor shall it constitute an assignment or grant to the other party or its representatives the rights and interests in relation to the trade secrets , trademarks , patents , know -how, or any other intellectual property authorized by a third party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'Confidential Information' with 'Protected Information,' creating a misalignment in terminology. The contradicted text refers to 'confidential information', but the definition has been altered. This introduces an in-text contradiction because the contract now uses a term that is no longer consistently defined or used, making it unclear whether the provisions in section 3.1 apply to 'Protected Information' or only 'Confidential Information.' This follows the definition of Misaligned Terminology, which states that in-text contradictions arise when a contract uses multiple terms interchangeably without defining them.

perturbation.original_text: Technical information or design concepts that are already or to be make public available, except those disclosed information by either Entrusting party or Re cipient or their representatives in violation of this agreement and without one party’s authorization.
perturbation.changed_text: Technical information or design concepts that are already or to be make public available, except those disclosed information by either Transferor or Transferee or their representatives in violation of this agreement and without one party’s permission.
perturbation.explanation: Replaced 'Entrusting party or Recipient' with 'Transferor or Transferee' but changed 'authorization' to 'permission'. This inconsistent use of 'authorization' versus 'permission' introduces ambiguity about what level of consent is required. In Section 1, the term 'authorization' is still used elsewhere, creating the conflict.
perturbation.location: Section 1 (Definition of Confidential Information, exception clause)
perturbation.contradicted_text: Neither Entrusting party nor Recipient party shall disclose or make public any confidential information to a third party(including the press) or otherwi se make use of the confidential information without the written approval or authorization of the other party; Both parties are obliged to instruct their representatives not to disclose or make public any confidential information to a third party(including the press) or otherwise make use of the confidential information;
perturbation.justification: The original text uses 'authorization,' while the changed text uses 'permission' instead of 'authorization'. The Contradicted Text uses 'authorization'. The Perturbation Explanation states that this inconsistent use introduces ambiguity about the level of consent required. This inconsistency directly clashes with the Contradicted Text, which clearly uses 'authorization,' creating a direct in-text contradiction as defined by the 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction' type. The ambiguity created by switching from 'authorization' to 'permission' makes the agreement unclear and therefore contradictory.

perturbation.original_text: Neither Entrusting party nor Recipient party shall disclose or make public any confidential information to a third party(including the press) or otherwi se make use of the confidential information without the written approval or authorization of the other party; Both parties are obliged to instruct their representatives not to disclose or make public any confidential information to a third party(including the press) or otherwise make use of the confidential information;
perturbation.changed_text: Neither Transferor nor Transferee party shall disclose or make public any protected information to a third party(including the press) or otherwi se make use of the protected information without the verbal approval or agreement of the other party; Both parties are obliged to instruct their representatives not to disclose or make public any protected information to a third party(including the press) or otherwise make use of the protected information;
perturbation.explanation: Replaced 'Entrusting party' and 'Recipient' with 'Transferor' and 'Transferee' and 'confidential information' with 'protected information,' and 'written approval or authorization' with 'verbal approval or agreement'. This creates conflict with the term authorization defined on the first perturbation in Section 1.
perturbation.contradicted_location: Section 1 (Definition of Confidential Information, exception clause)
perturbation.contradicted_text: Technical information or design concepts that are already or to be make public available, except those disclosed information by either Transferor or Transferee or their representatives in violation of this agreement and without one party’s permission.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'written approval or authorization' to 'verbal approval or agreement' introduces a conflict. The Contradicted Text discusses exceptions to information made public if there is no 'one party’s permission'. This suggests a need for documented, verifiable permission. By allowing verbal agreements, the change creates ambiguity and makes it difficult to determine if 'permission' was actually granted, thus contradicting the implied requirement of documentation in the Contradicted Text, as well as contradicting the term authorization defined on the first perturbation in Section 1, as stated in the explanation.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that the proceeding of the cooperative program ceases or either party quits the program with reasons , a party shall and shall urge its representatives to destroy or return to the other party all confidential information as well as all documents and materials and all duplicates thereof containing confidential information within five working days or upon the request of the other party at any time in writing duly addressed to the party opting out of this agreement. Nevertheless , the party possessing the confidential information may keep one piece of the duplicates of the documents or materials described above only for the purpose enshrined in Article 4 hereunder , without breaching other provisi ons of this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that the proceeding of the cooperative program ceases or either party quits the program with reasons , a party shall and shall urge its representatives to destroy or return to the other party all protected information as well as all documents and materials and all duplicates thereof containing protected information within two working days or upon the request of the other party at any time verbally addressed to the party opting out of this agreement. Nevertheless , the party possessing the protected information may keep one piece of the duplicates of the documents or materials described above only for the purpose enshrined in Article 4 hereunder , without breaching other provisi ons of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changed 'confidential information' to 'protected information', 'five working days' to 'two working days', and 'in writing duly addressed' to 'verbally addressed'. This creates a contradiction with the term 'five working days' on Section 1, and also contradicts with the term 'writing duly addressed', from Section 6.2.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6.2 Should the agreement be terminated, for points (ii) to (iv) above, either party s hall intimate the other party of such termination in writing duly addressed to the other party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies 'confidential information' to 'protected information' and changes the timeframe for returning information from 'five working days' to 'two working days'. Additionally, 'in writing duly addressed' is changed to 'verbally addressed'. Section 6.2 specifically requires termination notices to be 'in writing duly addressed'. Therefore, the change to 'verbally addressed' directly contradicts this requirement, creating a strong in-text contradiction. The change in timeframe from five to two days also constitutes a contradiction as previously established terms are not being maintained.

perturbation.original_text: This agreement shall be held in two copies of the same form. Each party shall preserve one copy with equal legal effect.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement shall be held in two copies of the same form. Each party shall preserve one copy without legal effect.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'with equal legal effect' introduces a contradiction in the enforceability of the agreement. This conflicts with the 'Preservation and Application of the Confidential Information' stated at section 2, which states that both parties have legal obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2. Preservation and Application of the Confidential Information 2.1 Both Entrusting party(Party A) or Recipient(Party B) have the right to preserve necessary confidential information, so as to make use of which in implementing bindi ng laws, regulations, and obligations under their cooperative programs.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes 'with equal legal effect' from the statement about the agreement copies, directly contradicting section 2.1. Section 2.1 states that both parties have the right to preserve confidential information and use it to implement binding laws, regulations, and obligations. If the agreement copies lack legal effect, as the changed text suggests, it conflicts with the binding obligations outlined in section 2.1, creating a strong in-text contradiction.
